answer:
Iqbal's early poem *Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet* (commonly known as *Tarana-e-Hindi*), from *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, celebrates India as a unified homeland through a chorus of historical and religious references spanning Chishti, Nanak, Tatars, Hijaz Arabs, Greeks, Turks, Persians, and biblical figures like Moses and Noah, all converging on "this land of mine" as a sacred, territorial space.[^1] This evokes a composite, patriotic "we" bound by geography and shared cultural heritage, positioning India as a cradle of spiritual and civilizational light.[^2]

In contrast, *Tarana-e-Milli* from *Bang-e-Dara Part 3* expands the "we" beyond territorial limits to a global Muslim community: "From Chin to Arab lands, our story flows, / The whole wide world's the home a Muslim knows."[^3] The poem emphasizes Islamic unity under tawhid (Oneness of God), guardianship of the Kaaba, historical conquests from Andalusia to the Tigris, and allegiance to the Prophet of Hijaz, framing the ummah as a borderless caravan sustained by faith rather than soil.[^4]

*Wataniyat*, immediately following in *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, explicitly rejects territorial nationalism as a modern "idol" that desecrates faith: "Of all these newer gods, the greatest is the Nation; / The robe it wears is Faith’s own final desecration."[^5] Iqbal urges Muslims to dismantle this "hollow idol," declaring "Your homeland is Islam" and "To be confined to one place is calamity," prioritizing prophetic universalism and brotherhood over geographic rivalry or state-bound identity.[^6]

This progression—from Indian territorial patriotism (*Tarana-e-Hindi*), to pan-Islamic communalism (*Tarana-e-Milli*), to outright repudiation of nationalism (*Wataniyat*)—mirrors Iqbal's evolving thought amid rising communal tensions, favoring spiritual universality over bounded loyalty. All appear in *Bang-e-Dara* (1924), with *Tarana-e-Hindi* in Part 1 and the others in Part 3.[^7]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 26 (*Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet*), [p-da6786](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786).

[^2]: Ibid., [paragraphs p-da6786 to p-a9f72d](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 23 (*Tarana-e-Milli*), [p-8a4681](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/translation/#p-8a4681).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-882645](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/translation/#p-882645).

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25 (*Wataniyat*), [p-79b1e9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^6]: Ibid., [paragraphs p-79b1e9 to p-a723b2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^7]: See *Bang-E-Dara* metadata across Parts 1 and 3.
